revision.comment: Bot: remove [[Category:Sn crud]] (crud category cleanup) [[github:EmmaLeonhart/shintowiki-scripts/actions/runs/23985667504|commit triggered pipeline]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:日高火防祭り]]
[[File:はやし屋台.JPG|thumb|250x250px|Hayashi Yatai (Yanagimachi Group)]] '''Hitaka Hibuse Festival''' (日高火防祭) is a fire prevention prayer festival held on the last Saturday of April in {{ill|Mizusawa|en|Mizusawa|qid=Q1114302|lt=Mizusawa|1=Mizusawa}}, {{ill|Ōshū, Iwate|en|Ōshū, Iwate|qid=Q386052|lt=Ōshū, Iwate|1=Ōshū, Iwate}}, {{ill|Iwate Prefecture|en|Iwate Prefecture|qid=Q48326|lt=Iwate Prefecture|1=Iwate Prefecture}}. With a history of over 300 years, it has been designated as an {{ill|Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja|無形民俗文化財|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Intangible Folk Cultural Property|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 民俗文化財|1=Folk Cultural Property|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q24895900}} of {{ill|Iwate Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties|ja|岩手県指定文化財一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Iwate Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties|1=Iwate Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11474689}}.<ref>[https://www.city.oshu.iwate.jp/site/kanko/5924.html Ōshū City - Hitaka Hibuse Festival]</ref>
== Overview == [[File:町印.JPG|thumb|Chōin (Ōmachi Group)]] The festival is a grand and colorful event modeled after {{ill|Gion Matsuri|en|Gion Matsuri|qid=Q979873|lt=Gion Festival|1=Gion Matsuri}} in {{ill|Kyoto Prefecture|en|Kyoto Prefecture|qid=Q120730|lt=Kyoto Prefecture|1=Kyoto Prefecture}}. Magnificent ''hayashi yatai'' floats parade through the town. Each of the nine participating town groups presents three types of floats: '''"Chōin" (Town Seal), "Uchibayashi" (Drumming Float), and "Hayashi Yatai" (Festival Float).''' However, the three newer groups that joined in the Meiji period—Ekimae Sanchō Group, Jōnai Group, and Yoshikōji Group—only present ''hayashi yatai'' without the ''chōin'' or ''uchibayashi'' floats.
The '''"Chōin" (町印)''' is a decorated float that bears a large sign with a single kanji character symbolizing the town, along with the town's name. It is adorned with elements such as {{ill|Well|en|Well|qid=Q43483|lt=Well|1=Well}}, {{ill|Horsehair Tassels|ja|馬簾|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Horsehair Tassels|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 纏|1=Matoi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2019498}}, {{ill|Fireball (festival decoration)|ja|火の玉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fireball (festival decoration)|1=Fireball (festival decoration)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11566835}}, and {{ill|Hishaku|en|Hishaku|qid=Q11533616|lt=Hishaku|1=Hishaku}}, leading each town’s procession. The origin of ''chōin'' dates back to the Edo period when the lord of {{ill|Mizusawa Castle|ja|水沢城|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mizusawa Castle|1=Mizusawa Castle|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11548846}} assigned a single character to each of the six original town groups: "仁" (Kawaguchi Group), "心" (Tatemachi Group), "火" (Yanagimachi Group), "防" (Ōmachi Group), "定" (Yokomachi Group), and "鎮" (Fukuro-machi Group). When combined, these characters form a phrase meaning "With benevolence and a calm heart, we prevent fires and ensure peace," emphasizing fire prevention awareness. [[File:打ちばやし.JPG|thumb|Uchibayashi (Yokomachi Group)]] The '''"Uchibayashi" (打ちばやし)''' refers to both the float and the music played within it. Also called ''Uchibayashi'' or ''Tottokome,'' it features a small shrine to a {{ill|Gongen|en|Gongen|qid=Q3080343|lt=Gongen|1=Gongen}} deity on its ceiling, with artificial {{ill|Weeping Cherry Blossom|ja|枝垂桜|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Weeping Cherry Blossom|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to シダレザクラ|1=Prunus pendula|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10879010}} flowers at the back. Smaller than the ''hayashi yatai'', it includes two flute players, two male drummers playing the {{ill|Ō-daiko|ja|大太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ō-daiko|1=Ō-daiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q123407539}}, and several young children playing the {{ill|Ko-daiko|ja|小太鼓|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ko-daiko|1=Ko-daiko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q123407541}}. The music, called ''Hitaka Bayashi,'' is composed of three segments (Shodan, Nidan, Sandan) and is said to resemble the {{ill|Sonata form|en|Sonata form|qid=Q212044|lt=Sonata form|1=Sonata form}} of Western music. Until the late Edo period, ''chōin'' and ''uchibayashi'' formed the core of the festival. [[File:はやし屋台見返り.JPG|thumb|133x133px|Hayashi Yatai Migaeri (Ōmachi Group)]] The '''"Hayashi Yatai" (はやし屋台)''' is an elaborate tiered float with {{ill|Karahafu|en|Karahafu|qid=Q977293|lt=Karahafu|1=Karahafu}} roofs, {{ill|Ramma|ja|欄間|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ramma|1=ranma|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q5862072}} panels painted in red and gold, and intricate carvings in bright colors. The railings have {{ill|Giboshi|en|Giboshi|qid=Q3105399|lt=Gibōshi|1=Giboshi}} finials, and lanterns are placed on the float. The back (''migaeri'') is adorned with artificial peonies and weeping cherry blossoms. Only Yoshikōji Group retains traditional side panels with {{ill|Soba|en|Soba|qid=Q753910|lt=Soba|1=Soba}} husks and {{ill|Chrysanthemum|ja|菊|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Chrysanthemum|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to キク|1=Chrysanthemum × morifolium|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q15530181}} motifs for protection against misfortune. The fabric drapes and performers' attire feature the ''Kaku Mokko'' (角木瓜) crest, associated with {{ill|Tokiwazu|ja|常磐津|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tokiwazu|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 常磐津節|1=Tokiwazu-bushi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11481587}}, although the connection is unclear.
The musical performance features two flute players leading the melody, with young women known as "Oningyō-san" (お人形さん, "Doll Girls") playing small taiko drums (15–20 participants), and five young women called "Oyome-san" (お嫁さん, "Bride Girls") playing {{ill|Shamisen|en|Shamisen|qid=Q241337|lt=Shamisen|1=Shamisen}} under the guidance of a master. Drums were historically included but are now only decorative elements. The collective term for the festival music played on both ''uchibayashi'' and ''hayashi yatai'' is ''Hitaka Bayashi''.
Until the 1960s, most floats were carried by hand, requiring dozens of men singing {{ill|Kiyari|ja|木遣り歌|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiyari|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 木遣|1=kiyari|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11519400}} as they bore the weight. Due to manpower shortages, most floats now use wheeled carts. To preserve tradition, some floats still include decorative carrying poles, and ''yusuri'' (a maneuver where floats turn to face spectators or shops) is now performed using a rotating platform.
=== Participating ''Hayashi Yatai'' Groups (Melody Type) === (Note: Groups marked with ※ also have ''chōin'' and ''uchibayashi''.) * Yokomachi Group – ''Ichikoe Kuzushi'' ※ * Fukuro-machi Group – ''Ichikoe'' ※ * Ekimae Sanchō Group – ''Ichikoe'' * Kawaguchi Group – ''Tsurubei'' ※ * Yanagimachi Group – ''Kenbayashi Kuzushi'' ※ * Jōnai Group – ''Kanran'' * Tatemachi Group – ''Matsu no Midori'' ※ * Yoshikōji Group – ''Gion Bayashi'' * Ōmachi Group – ''Gion Bayashi'' ※
== Various Events ==
=== Fuekon-sai & First Performance Ceremony === Held on January 22 (the original festival date in the lunar calendar), this event includes a ritual at {{ill|Hitaka Shrine|ja|日高神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitaka Shrine|1=Hitaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11510848}}, where flutists purify their instruments with sacred sake.
=== Formal Attire Presentation & Triple Ensemble Performance === Held one week before the festival, this ceremony at Hitaka Shrine features the presentation of traditional ''kamishimo'' attire for flutists and an ensemble performance of the three musical elements (flute, shamisen, and taiko).
=== Main Festival === The festival begins in the morning with a safety prayer at Hitaka Shrine. Each town’s floats parade through the town, stopping at the shrine entrance for a ''Yōhai-shiki'' (遥拝式) ceremony where all floats line up and face the shrine to receive blessings. The procession then moves to the city hall, where performances take place.
At 1:15 PM, led by firefighters displaying {{ill|Matoi|en|Matoi|qid=Q2019498|lt=Matoi|1=Matoi}}, the parade winds through historical town districts, including stops at the statue of Sasaki Sagohei, the founder of Mizusawa’s firefighting tradition.
At 6:40 PM, the festival reaches its climax with the '''"Soroi-uchi" (揃い打ち)''' and '''"Ai-uchi" (相打ち)'''. In ''Soroi-uchi,'' all nine ''hayashi yatai'' gather to perform simultaneously at Mizusawa Station Street. ''Ai-uchi'' follows at the intersection in front of the {{ill|Maple (disambiguation)|en|Maple (disambiguation)|qid=Q412554|lt=Maple (commercial facility)|1=Maple (disambiguation)}}, where the main town’s float challenges others in a musical duel, culminating in a ritual of mutual respect.
== History == The festival's origins are unclear, but two main theories exist: 1. Lord {{ill|Date Munekage|ja|伊達宗景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Munekage|1=Date Munekage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11380725}} established the festival after witnessing the destruction of the {{ill|Great Fire of Meireki|en|Great Fire of Meireki|qid=Q1336847|lt=Meireki Fire|1=Great Fire of Meireki}} in 1657 in Edo and initiated a fire prevention ritual at Hitaka Shrine. 2. In 1735, a major fire destroyed 168 homes in Mizusawa, prompting Lord {{ill|Date Murakage|ja|伊達村景|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Date Murakage|1=Date Murakage|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11231760}} to send Sasaki Sagohei to Edo to learn firefighting techniques, leading to the creation of the ''Gaein Group'' fire brigade, celebrated with this festival.
== Related Articles == * {{ill|Hitaka Shrine|ja|日高神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hitaka Shrine|1=Hitaka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11510848}} * {{ill|List of festivals in Japan|en|List of festivals in Japan|qid=Q10312376|lt=Festivals of Japan|1=List of festivals in Japan}}
== External Links == * [https://www.city.oshu.iwate.jp/site/kanko/5924.html Ōshū City - Hitaka Hibuse Festival] )]][[File:Okunitama-jinja-30.jpg|thumb|230px|{{ill|Ōkunitama Shrine|lt=Ōkunitama Shrine|lt=Okunitama Shrine|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Okunitama Shrine|qq=}} Reisai (part of {{ill|Kurayami Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kurayami Festival|qq=}})]]
'''Spring Matsuri''' (春祭)<ref name="Daijisen">『{{ill|Daijisen|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Daijisen|qq=}}』</ref><ref>Heibonsha 『{{ill|MyPedia|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/MyPedia|qq=}}』</ref><ref>Heibonsha 『{{ill|Sekai Daihyakka Jiten|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sekai Daihyakka Jiten|qq=}}』, 2nd Edition</ref><ref>Shogakukan 『{{ill|Nipponica|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Nipponica|qq=}}』</ref>, also called '''Spring Festival''' or '''Haru no Matsuri''' (はるまつり), is a general term for {{ill|festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/festivals|qq=}} held in the {{ill|spring|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/spring|qq=}}.
== Seasonal Word == As a {{ill|kigo|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/kigo|qq=}}, the term '''Spring Matsuri''' (春祭り) represents a seasonal word for spring (either for {{ill|early spring|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/early spring|qq=}} or the three months of spring, depending on the {{ill|saijiki|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/saijiki|qq=}}).<ref name="Kigosai">{{Cite web|和書|date=2011-02-16 |url=http://kigosai.sub.jp/kigo500e/341.html |title=春祭 |work=Kigosai Saijiki |publisher=Kigosai Saijiki Association |accessdate=2018-02-17]]</ref> It falls under the classification of {{ill|events|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/events|qq=}} or human affairs.<ref>"Events" and "human affairs" refer here to activities performed by humans.</ref> In terms of seasonal words, it encompasses festivals held during the spring season.<ref name="Kigosai" /> * Example: The '''Spring Matsuri''' begins with the sprouting of old mulberries – {{ill|Shūson Mizuhara|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shūson Mizuhara|qq=}}<ref name="Daijisen" /> * Example: '''Spring Matsuri''' beneath the mountains, few fresh monpe in sight – {{ill|Hakyō Ishida|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hakyō Ishida|qq=}} * Example: Valleys open to the sight of Norikura at '''Spring Matsuri''' – {{ill|Fura Maeda|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fura Maeda|qq=}} 『Hida Tsumugi』 (published 1947)<ref name="Kigosai" /> * Example: Shellfish alive in a tank at '''Spring Matsuri''' – Yaehiro Hiroe 『Chikuma Literary Anthology』<ref name="Kigosai" />There are no sub-seasonal words for it. While no directly related seasonal words are listed, many spring festivals, such as the {{ill|Kinensai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinensai|qq=}}, are seasonal words, which naturally relate to Spring Matsuri.
== Notes and References == {{Reflist}}
== See Also == * '''[[Spring Matsuri]]''' ─ {{ill|Summer Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Matsuri|qq=}} ─ {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Matsuri|qq=}} ─ {{ill|Winter Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Matsuri|qq=}} * Festivals as seasonal words: '''Spring Matsuri''' ─ Festivals ({{ill|Summer Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Matsuri|qq=}}) ─ {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Matsuri|qq=}} * {{ill|Kinensai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kinensai|qq=}} * {{ill|Easter|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Easter|qq=}} * {{ill|Martenitsa|en|Martenitsa}} (Martisor, Martinki) – A spring festival in Eastern Europe, inscribed in 2017 on the {{ill|UNESCO Intangible Cultural Heritage Lists|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Martenitsa|qq=}} * {{ill|Hidrellez|en|Hıdırellez}} – A spring festival in Turkey and Macedonia, inscribed in 2017 on the Intangible Cultural Heritage list * {{ill|Walpurgis Night|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hidrellez|qq=}} * {{ill|Passover|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Passover|qq=}} * {{ill|The Rite of Spring|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/The Rite of Spring|qq=}} – Ballet music by Igor Stravinsky * {{ill|Flower Festival (Folklore)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Flower Festival (Folklore)|qq=}} – Andean folklore * {{ill|Reisai|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Reisai|qq=}} * {{ill|Cherry blossom festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Cherry blossom festival|qq=}} * {{ill|Maruyama Park (Akitakata)|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Maruyama Park (Akitakata)|qq=}} * {{ill|Taiko no Tatsujin|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Taiko no Tatsujin|qq=}} – Includes the software titled 『Taiko no Tatsujin Dokki! Shinkyoku Darake no Spring Matsuri』
{{Event-stub}}
* {{ill|Summer Matsuri|lt=Summer Matsuri|lt=Summer festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Autumn Matsuri|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Winter Matsuri|lt=Winter Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en}} * * {{ill|Community building|en}}
*

characters]]
